questions.questionText: The most common cause of nosocomial (hospital-acquired) infections is:
questions.optionB: Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and Gram-negative bacilli (E. coli, Pseudomonas, Klebsiella)
questions.explanation: Nosocomial (HAI) infections: most common causes vary by site — UTI: E. coli (most common HAI overall, catheter-associated), Surgical site: S. aureus, Pneumonia: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (VAP), Bloodstream: coagulase-negative Staphylococci (catheter-related), S. aureus. MRSA: major nosocomial pathogen. Prevention: hand hygiene (most effective measure), catheter care bundles, contact precautions. C. difficile: most common cause of antibiotic-associated diarrhea (nosocomial). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Nosocomial Infections.

questions.optionB: Chlamydia and Rickettsia (cannot be cultured on artificial media, require living cells)
questions.explanation: Obligate intracellular: cannot grow on artificial media (need host cell for energy/nutrients). Chlamydia: 'energy parasites' (use host ATP), elementary body (infectious, extracellular), reticulate body (replicating, intracellular). Rickettsia: obligate intracellular, transmitted by arthropod vectors (ticks, lice, fleas, mites). Exception: Coxiella burnetii (survives extracellularly). Chlamydia trachomatis: trachoma, STDs, lymphogranuloma venereum. Culture: cell culture (McCoy cells for Chlamydia, Vero cells for Rickettsia). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Obligate Intracellular.

questions.questionText: Quellung reaction (capsule swelling test) is used to identify:
questions.optionB: Encapsulated bacteria (Streptococcus pneumoniae — most commonly tested, also Haemophilus, Klebsiella, Neisseria)
questions.explanation: Quellung reaction: mixing bacteria with type-specific anticapsular antibody → capsule appears to swell (actually increased refractility due to antibody binding). Used for: rapid identification and serotyping of encapsulated organisms, especially Streptococcus pneumoniae (83+ serotypes). Also used for: H. influenzae type b, Klebsiella, N. meningitidis. Capsule is the most important virulence factor of S. pneumoniae (antiphagocytic). Capsule is target of conjugate vaccines. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Quellung Reaction.

questions.questionText: The MOST sensitive test for syphilis screening is:
questions.optionB: VDRL/RPR (non-treponemal tests — used for screening, can have false positives)
questions.explanation: Syphilis diagnosis: two-step approach. Screening: non-treponemal tests — VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Lab) or RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin). Detect anti-cardiolipin antibodies (reagin). Sensitive but not specific (false positives: SLE, pregnancy, viral infections). Confirmation: treponemal tests — FTA-ABS (Fluorescent Treponemal Antibody Absorption, most specific), TPHA. VDRL: quantitative → used to monitor treatment response (titer falls). Dark-field microscopy: direct visualization of spirochete in primary chancre. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Syphilis Diagnosis.

questions.questionText: Widal test is used for the serological diagnosis of:
questions.optionB: Enteric fever (typhoid — detects antibodies against O and H antigens of Salmonella typhi)
questions.explanation: Widal test: tube/slide agglutination test detecting antibodies against: O antigen (somatic, heat-stable LPS — IgM, appears early, suggests recent infection) and H antigen (flagellar, heat-labile protein — IgG, persists longer). Rising titer in paired sera (>4-fold) is more diagnostic than single titer. Limitations: false positive (cross-reactions, prior vaccination), false negative (early disease, antibiotics). Blood culture: gold standard for typhoid (first week). Stool culture: positive in 2nd-3rd week. Reference: Ananthanarayan — Widal Test.
questions.conceptTag: Widal test typhoid O H antigens
